title: Fix rotted chromium pin in Dockerfile blocking all CodeBuild deploys
description: CodeBuild (image build on push to main) has been FAILED since ~07:03 PDT 2026-07-12 (builds 71-75). Root cause: Dockerfile:34 pins chromium=147.0.7727.137-1~deb12u1 exactly, and Debian dropped that point release from the live bookworm repo -> 'Version not found' (apt exit 100) -> docker build fails -> no image pushed -> in-build cdk deploy never runs -> blocks ALL infra+image changes (incl. the rosterbot-00j BuildNotify rule/lambda, which therefore don't exist and never notify). Fix: bump ARG CHROMIUM_VERSION to current bookworm main (150.0.7871.100-1~deb12u1, arm64 confirmed) and CI-validate on Graviton via the existing headless smoke test. Note: 150.0.7871.46 previously SIGTRAP'd on Graviton; .100 is a newer point release under validation.
close_reason: Shipped: #42 (chromium 150.0.7871.100, builds 76/77 green) + #41 (BuildNotify rule live, notifications firing). Re-closing on main dolt branch.

title: GetTeamGS mis-derives period number for every day inside a merged multi-day scoring period
description:
internal/fantrax/gs_check.go's GetTeamGS builds a periodFor closure (lines ~176-184) that resolves each day in its walk via AnchorPeriodForDate(today, currentPeriod, d) -- plain day-by-day anchor arithmetic assuming exactly one scoring period per calendar day. But the ScoringPeriod (sp) GetTeamGS is called with can itself be a merged multi-day period: TestFindCurrentPeriod_MergedAllStarBreakPeriod (internal/fantrax/gs_check_test.go) already fixtures this exact case -- period 16 spans 2026-07-13 to 2026-07-26 (14 calendar days under one period number) for the All-Star break. That merged period starts TOMORROW relative to when this issue was filed (2026-07-12).

GetTeamGS backs both internal/gscheck.RunGSCheck (league-wide GS violation detection, gs-check command) and cmd/optimize.go's GS-budget gate (weekly game-start limit awareness, optimize command when GS_TRACKING_ENABLED=true) -- both live production paths. There is no existing test coverage for GetTeamGS's walk across a merged period.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Use the periods fixture from TestFindCurrentPeriod_MergedAllStarBreakPeriod: period 16 spans StartDate=2026-07-13, EndDate=2026-07-26; period 17 starts 2026-07-27.
2. Call GetTeamGS with sp=period 16 (the merged period), today=2026-07-27 (the day after it ends), and a GetCurrentPeriod() result of 17 (so periodFor uses AnchorPeriodForDate(2026-07-27, 17, d) for each day d in the walk).
3. Observe periodFor(2026-07-13) = 17 + (13-27) = 3, periodFor(2026-07-14) = 4, periodFor(2026-07-20) = 10, periodFor(2026-07-25) = 15 -- all wrong; only periodFor(2026-07-26) = 16 (the day nearest the anchor) happens to be correct.
4. Each wrong period number is a real period elsewhere in the season (e.g. period 3 is an actual April date), so getPlayerGSSnapshotForPeriod(teamID, period) silently returns a real but unrelated day's roster/GS snapshot instead of erroring -- the corruption is silent, not a crash.
design: Thread the already-fetched periods []ScoringPeriod list into GetTeamGS (both callers -- gscheck.go and cmd/optimize.go's GS-budget block -- already have it in scope after rosterbot-z3b) and replace the periodFor closure's AnchorPeriodForDate/PeriodForDate pair with fantrax.ResolvePeriod(periods, currentPeriod, seasonStart, today, d) -- the same periods-list-first resolver rosterbot-z3b introduced for the apply path. This requires changing GetTeamGS's signature to accept periods (or a resolver function) as a parameter.
acceptance_criteria: A test using the existing TestFindCurrentPeriod_MergedAllStarBreakPeriod-style fixture (period 16 spanning multiple calendar days) verifies GetTeamGS's day-by-day walk resolves every day in the merged span to the correct period number, not just the day nearest the anchor. Both GetTeamGS callers (gscheck.RunGSCheck, cmd/optimize.go's GS-budget block) pass their already-fetched periods list through. Existing GetTeamGS tests continue to pass.
notes: Fixed: GetTeamGS's periodFor closure replaced with pure gsPeriodWalk, resolving the whole day range via ResolvePeriod up front instead of bare per-day anchor arithmetic. Both callers (internal/gscheck, cmd/optimize.go GS-budget block) pass their already-fetched periods list through. Regression test (period-16 merged fixture, all 14 days) added; reviewer independently reproduced the old formula to confirm the test is non-tautological (13/14 days were wrong before the fix). Shipped 2026-07-12, one day before this season's period-16 merge opens.
close_reason: gsPeriodWalk resolves the merged-period walk via ResolvePeriod; code-reviewed, tested, shipped ahead of period 16 opening 2026-07-13.

title: Stop .beads jsonl merge conflicts on every PR (union merge driver)
description: Tracked passive exports .beads/issues.jsonl + interactions.jsonl are regenerated per-branch by beads hooks, so nearly every PR hits a textual merge conflict on them. Real sync is Dolt over sync.remote git refs; jsonl is export-only (no-db:false). Fix: commit .gitattributes with 'merge=union' on both files -> git stops raising conflicts, post-merge hook re-exports clean from Dolt. Verified via git check-attr (merge: union).
close_reason: Landed in #43 (commit 7a96bdb): .gitattributes sets merge=union on .beads/issues.jsonl + interactions.jsonl, verified via git check-attr

title: Add Pushover notification for successful and unsuccessful CodeBuilds in AWS
description: We need notifications of successful nd unsuccessful CodeBuilds from merging PRs or pushing to main directly which should already trigger a code build.
close_reason: Shipped: #42 (chromium 150.0.7871.100, builds 76/77 green) + #41 (BuildNotify rule live, notifications firing). Re-closing on main dolt branch.

title: Validate whether hitter/pitcher YTD blend beats a bounded window or no-blend baseline
description:
Two independent audit agents (fantasy-baseball-model-auditor and fantasy-baseball-strategist, 2026-07-10) independently flagged the same risk: internal/projections/blended.go and pitcher_blended.go blend the base FanGraphs RoS projection (depthcharts-ros, which already incorporates 2026 YTD performance with proper regression) with RAW, unregressed season-to-date FP/G. At ~90 GP for a regular, seasonWeight=approxPA/(approxPA+250) is already ~58%. This risks double-counting the same in-season signal and over-reacting to hot/cold streaks.

I extended cmd/backtest.go's --recency-experiment harness (previously ytd/w14/w30/decay21 only) with a 'base' variant (no blend at all, pure depthcharts-ros) to isolate the effect, and ran it live:

  --matchup (last completed week, 2026-06-29..2026-07-05, 7 days):
    base    realized=387.0  meanGap=-19.14
    ytd     realized=405.0  meanGap=-16.57  (current production strategy — WON this week)
    w14     realized=340.0  meanGap=-25.86
    w30     realized=396.0  meanGap=-17.86
    decay21 realized=389.0  meanGap=-18.86

  --weeks 4 (2026-06-15..2026-07-09, 25 days):
    base    realized=1252.0 meanGap=-23.76
    ytd     realized=1226.0 meanGap=-24.80  (current production strategy — worse than base AND w30)
    w14     realized=1151.0 meanGap=-27.80  (clearly worst)
    w30     realized=1261.0 meanGap=-23.40  (best of all 5 variants)
    decay21 realized=1217.0 meanGap=-25.16

Over the longer window, current production (ytd/season-to-date) is beaten by both a bounded 30-day window AND a no-blend baseline. The single-week result flips the other way, consistent with a real-but-modest, sample-dependent effect rather than a huge bug. 25 hitter-days is still a small sample for a strategy decision that affects every lineup every day.
acceptance_criteria: Run --recency-experiment over a much longer window (full season to date, or --weeks 8+) to get a stable read. If w30 (or base) continues to beat ytd, either (a) switch BlendedSource's recent-stats input from unbounded season-to-date to a bounded rolling window, or (b) blend against a preseason (independent) projection instead of an in-season-updated RoS system, or (c) lower the recent-weight ceiling. Extend the same experiment to pitchers (pitcher_blended.go) — the current harness is hitters-only. Update CLAUDE.md's Blended scoring section once a decision is made.
notes: Tooling change (the 'base' variant + HasProjDiag n/a-vs-0.00 fix in internal/backtest/strategy_replay.go and cmd/backtest.go) already committed. This issue is about acting on what the tool shows, not about the tool itself.

comments.author: Jon Nixon
comments.text:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Determine whether the production hitter/pitcher recent-performance blend is actually improving lineup decisions versus simpler alternatives, and act on the finding.

**Current behavior:**
The blend combines the season-long base projection with the player's raw season-to-date performance, weighted increasingly toward recent performance as the season progresses. A strategy-comparison tool exists that can replay several alternative strategies (no blend at all, various rolling windows) against actual historical lineup outcomes. A short initial comparison run showed the current strategy losing to both a no-blend baseline and a bounded 30-day window over a 4-week sample, but winning over a single-week sample — not enough data to be confident either way.

**Desired behavior:**
A confident, data-backed answer on whether the current blend strategy is the best of the available options for both hitters and pitchers, backed by a much larger sample than what's been checked so far, followed by either confirming the current approach or switching to whichever alternative the data supports.

**Key interfaces:**
- The existing strategy-comparison tool already supports adding/removing named strategies to compare; extending it to pitchers (currently hitters-only) is straightforward if needed.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] The comparison has been run over a much larger sample (full season to date at minimum) for hitters.
- [ ] A decision has been made and recorded: keep the current strategy, or switch to a specific alternative, with the reasoning captured.
- [ ] If a change is made, it's verified not to regress recent lineup outcomes before being treated as the new default.

**Why this needs a human, not an agent:**
This changes a formula that determines every lineup's point projections, every day, for the rest of the season — a live, standing decision with real competitive stakes, not a self-contained bug fix. "How much data is enough to be confident" and "which alternative to prefer if several look close" are judgment calls the maintainer should make directly rather than delegate. An agent CAN be asked to do the mechanical part (re-run the comparison tool over a longer window and report the numbers back) as a preliminary, lower-stakes step if useful — that sub-task would itself be ready-for-agent even though the decision that follows isn't.

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the blend formula's underlying shrinkage math (the plate-appearance-based weighting curve itself) — that's a separate, deeper design question from "which recency window to blend."

title: Remove GS_MAX/GS_MIN static env vars; source everything live from Fantrax
description: Now that GS budget/gscheck fetch real limits live from Fantrax (rosterbot-r10), the GS_MAX/GS_MIN numeric env vars only ever served as a fallback value + feature toggle. Replace with a single GS_TRACKING_ENABLED boolean toggle; on live-fetch failure, send a Pushover alert (PUSHOVER_USER_KEY) instead of falling back to a stale static number. Also remove GS_MAX/GS_MIN from infra/infra.go's ECS secrets and the AWS SSM parameters. Discovered along the way: internal/lineupapi (used by the Lambda in lambda/) transitively imports internal/fantrax, and lambda/go.mod had no replace pin for the go-fantrax fork branch, so cdk synth was already broken on main since PR #29 merged — fixed as part of this work.
notes: PR: https://github.com/nixon-commits/rosterbot/pull/35 (branch worktree-gs-tracking-toggle). Also fixes an unrelated but blocking cdk-deploy failure (lambda/go.mod missing go-fantrax fork pin) that's been broken since PR #29 merged ~9am.
close_reason: GS_TRACKING_ENABLED toggle deployed and verified healthy in production (25 runs, 0 failures since deploy). Fixed unrelated blocking cdk-deploy bug (lambda/go.mod). GS_MAX/GS_MIN SSM parameters deleted, confirmed no remaining references anywhere in the codebase.

title: Fetch live Fantrax GS position limits instead of static GS_MAX/GS_MIN
description: Static GS_MAX/GS_MIN env vars diverge from Fantrax's real per-period limits whenever a scoring period spans more than one calendar week (confirmed: period 1 real 17/21, period 16 real 15/19, both vs configured 10/12). Add go-fantrax GetTeamRosterPositionCounts endpoint, wire internal/fantrax.GetGSLimits, update cmd/optimize.go and internal/gscheck to use it with env-var fallback. See docs/superpowers/specs/2026-07-06-fantrax-gs-limits-design.md.
close_reason: Live Fantrax GS limits wired into optimize + gscheck with env-var fallback; verified directly against the live league for periods 1/15/16/17 (16=15/19, the All-Star break value). go-fantrax PR: https://github.com/pmurley/go-fantrax/pull/7. Follow-up rosterbot-6ds tracks post-merge go.mod cleanup.

title: Consolidate period resolution into one Period Resolver module
description: Four independent derivations of "what scoring period is date X" exist with no single module owning the answer: PeriodForDate (season-start day math), AnchorPeriodForDate (anchor on todays current period), GetMatchupWeekNumberForDate (positional count over matchupWeekRanges, no gap check) in internal/fantrax/matchup_weeks.go, and period.Number parsed directly from Fantrax caption text in internal/gscheck. cmd/optimize.go and internal/gscheck each pick their own derivation for the GS-budget block. Architecture review (improve-codebase-architecture, 2026-07-12) flagged this as a shallow-seam problem: the same domain concept (period for a date) is computed independently at four call sites, and two of those derivations have already been caught disagreeing in production-adjacent testing (see rosterbot-2bl, rosterbot-ren).
design: Consolidate into one Period Resolver module in internal/fantrax with a single interface for resolving a date to its authoritative scoring period. cmd/optimize.go, internal/gscheck, matchup_weeks.go, and recent_stats.go all resolve through it instead of re-deriving. AnchorPeriodForDate-style anchoring on todays current period stays as the near-today strategy inside the resolver; PeriodForDate-style day math stays as the fallback inside the resolver -- the point is one place picks between them, not four.
acceptance_criteria: Single ResolvePeriod-style entry point exists in internal/fantrax. cmd/optimize.go GS-budget block and internal/gscheck.RunGSCheck both resolve period through it (no independent re-derivation). The scenario rosterbot-2bl describes (matchupWeekRanges position count silently desyncing from the real ScoringPeriod number on a back-to-back rematch) is provably impossible after the change, not just verified-absent-this-season. rosterbot-rens near-today Class A behavior is unaffected; the resolver is the structural host the season-wide historical date-to-period map (Class B) can be built into as a follow-on.
notes:
Implemented as fantrax.ResolvePeriod(periods, currentPeriod, seasonStart, today, date) in internal/fantrax/period_resolver.go -- three-tier fallback (authoritative periods list -> anchor arithmetic -> day math). cmd/optimize.go's lineup-apply path (former local resolveDatePeriod, now deleted) migrated to call it. Tests moved from cmd/optimize_period_test.go to internal/fantrax/period_resolver_test.go, closer to the logic.

Scoping decision (reviewed 2026-07-12, code-reviewer subagent): internal/gscheck.RunGSCheck and cmd/optimize.go's GS-budget block were deliberately NOT routed through ResolvePeriod. Both already source period from the same GetScoringPeriodsAndTeams() authoritative list via role-specific selectors (FindJustEndedPeriod, FindCurrentPeriod) for different questions than "what period is date X" -- they agree with ResolvePeriod's tier 1 by construction. Routing the safety-critical GS-budget gate through a function with weaker anchor/day-math fallback tiers would have been a behavior change to already-shipped, already-verified code, not a genuine dedup. Reviewer confirmed this reasoning holds.

Review also surfaced (and this PR's CLAUDE.md now documents accurately) a separate, real, NOT-yet-fixed bug in internal/fantrax/gs_check.go's GetTeamGS: its internal periodFor closure uses day-by-day anchor arithmetic that mis-derives the period number for every day within a merged multi-day scoring period except the one nearest the anchor (see TestFindCurrentPeriod_MergedAllStarBreakPeriod's period-16 fixture, 2026-07-13 to 2026-07-26 -- this season's real All-Star break, starting the day after this issue closed). Filed as a separate urgent follow-on rather than folded into this issue's scope.
close_reason: fantrax.ResolvePeriod implemented, tested, code-reviewed, and wired into cmd/optimize.go's apply path. See notes for the deliberate GS-budget/gscheck scoping decision and a newly-discovered follow-on bug filed separately.

title: Build exact date→period map for historical recap/backtest (period-drift Class B)
description:
Fantrax inserts extra daily scoring periods mid-season (doubleheader / postponed-game makeups), so PeriodForDate(seasonStart, date) -- which assumes one period per calendar day -- drifts behind Fantrax's authoritative numbering by the insertion count. Confirmed in 2026: matched through 06-07, off by one by 06-23.

Class A (near-today, action-affecting) is already fixed: fantrax.AnchorPeriodForDate(today, currentPeriod, date) anchors on Fantrax's authoritative current period, used by cmd/optimize.go's resolveDatePeriod and internal/fantrax/gs_check.go's GetTeamGS.

Class B (historical reporting) is still open: DailyFantasyPoints (internal/fantrax/daily_fpts.go, used by recap/recap-site/backtest/grade) and GetTeamPitcherStarts (internal/fantrax/pitcher_starts.go, used by recap/recap-site) still use season-start day math. A naive today-anchor fix would break early pre-insertion weeks in the weekly recap-site full rebuild (it re-derives every completed week each Monday) -- neither a season-start anchor nor a today anchor is globally correct across insertion boundaries. Needs an authoritative season-wide date-to-daily-period map.

Migrated from .scratch/period-drift/issues/01-historical-period-date-map.md (predates the bd migration, never ported over).

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run 'go run . recap-site --out dist' (or 'go run . backtest --dates 2026-06-01:2026-06-30') covering a range that spans an insertion boundary, e.g. 2026-06-07 through 2026-06-23.
2. For each date, compare the period number DailyFantasyPoints derives via season-start day math against Fantrax's authoritative scoring-period number for that same date (visible via the Fantrax UI or getStandings' period captions).
3. Observe the two agree through 2026-06-07 and are off by one starting 2026-06-23, after Fantrax inserted an extra daily period for a doubleheader/postponed-game makeup.
4. Days/GS attribution in the affected week's recap or backtest report will be pulled from the wrong scoring period as a result.
acceptance_criteria:
recap-site full rebuild: every week's daily FP/GS attribution matches Fantrax-authoritative scoring across insertion boundaries (week-1 and a post-insertion week both correct in one build). backtest --dates <historical-range> grades the correct days. No per-day extra network calls on the hot path (map must be cached, season-stable).

Investigation notes: the roster API (getTeamRosterInfo) addresses purely by period number, no date param -- the period's date may live in the opaque TeamRosterResponse DisplayedSelections/MiscData blobs (parsed as map[string]interface{} in go-fantrax), needs spelunking, possibly a go-fantrax change to expose it. Alternative: probe period snapshots to discover insertion boundaries (offset is a non-decreasing step function), then encode the piecewise map.
notes: From memory period-drift-2026 and the pre-bd .scratch/ tracker. Class A fix reference: cmd/optimize.go resolveDatePeriod, internal/fantrax/gs_check.go GetTeamGS, fantrax.AnchorPeriodForDate.

title: Team-abbreviation normalization inconsistently applied between projection and schedule paths
description: internal/projections normalizes the Fantrax team via NormalizeTeam before using it as a lookup key (projKey), but the schedule/probable-starter path compares the RAW p.MLBTeam field against normalized map keys (cmd/optimize.go:527,558; internal/optimizer/lineup.go:260). Verified live on 2026-07-10 that Fantrax currently returns canonical abbreviations for all 30 teams (including the recent OAK->ATH rename) matching NormalizeTeam's output, so this is NOT currently causing incorrect behavior. But it's a latent coupling: if Fantrax or MLB statsapi ever diverges on an abbreviation again (exactly the class of change OAK->ATH already was), the schedule-matching path would silently mark that team as 'no game' and bench every one of its hitters, with no error or warning surfaced anywhere.
acceptance_criteria: Normalize once at construction time in internal/fantrax/client.go, at the ~3 sites that set MLBTeam from MLBTeamShortName/TeamShortName, so every downstream consumer (projection AND schedule paths) sees the same canonical form. NormalizeTeam is already idempotent so this is safe to apply at the earliest point without touching the projection path's existing correctness.
notes: From fantasy-baseball-strategist review, 2026-07-10 (finding #4). Defensive hardening, not an active bug.

comments.author: Jon Nixon
comments.text:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Team abbreviations are normalized to a canonical form in one part of the lineup pipeline (projections) but compared in raw form in another part (schedule/probable-starter matching), which currently produces correct results only because the upstream data happens to already be in canonical form — a latent fragility rather than an active bug.

**Current behavior:**
Player records carry a team abbreviation sourced from the roster provider. One consumer normalizes this abbreviation before using it as a lookup key; another consumer (matching a player's team against today's game schedule) compares the raw, un-normalized abbreviation directly against schedule data. Today these happen to agree for all 30 teams, so nothing is currently broken. If the roster provider and the schedule data source ever diverge on an abbreviation for the same team again (this has happened before with at least one real-world team rebrand), the schedule-matching consumer would silently treat every player on that team as not playing that day, with no error or warning.

**Desired behavior:**
Team abbreviations should be normalized once, at the point they're first read from the roster provider, so every downstream consumer — including the schedule/probable-starter matching path — sees the same canonical form without needing to remember to normalize it themselves.

**Key interfaces:**
- The roster-provider client code that first populates a player's team-abbreviation field (there are a small number of call sites that do this from slightly different upstream field names).
- The existing team-normalization function is already idempotent, so applying it earlier is safe even where it's redundant with later normalization.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] All call sites that populate a player's team abbreviation from upstream data apply the existing normalization function before storing it.
- [ ] The projection-lookup path (which already normalizes) is unaffected — applying normalization twice is a no-op.
- [ ] New test coverage exists that would fail today's raw-comparison path (using a synthetic abbreviation mismatch) and passes once normalization happens at the source.

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the normalization mapping itself (which abbreviations map to which canonical form) — only where/when normalization is applied.

title: GS budget gate ceil-rounds fractional future SP demand, can over-suppress today's certain starts
description: internal/optimizer/gs_budget.go:164, applyGSGate: estCount := int(math.Ceil(futureEstimated)) turns a fractional rotation-math estimate for a future day without announced probables (e.g. 0.2 expected starts) into a full placeholder entry valued at the roster-wide mean SP projection, competing at FULL value against today's confirmed starters in the value-ranked gate. Combined with valuing every estimated future start at the roster-SP mean (not discounted for its lower certainty), this overstates future claims on the remaining weekly GS budget and can bench a certain, real start today in favor of a future start that may never materialize (probables not yet announced, rainout, roster move, etc). Only fires when GS_TRACKING_ENABLED and the weekly budget is genuinely tight, so scoped, but real when it does.
acceptance_criteria: Value estimated future starts at their fractional expectation rather than ceil-rounding to full placeholder entries (e.g. weight the placeholder's contribution to the ranking by the fractional amount rather than creating a full extra competing entry), and/or add a small certainty premium to today's confirmed starts so near-ties resolve toward the sure thing. The existing exact-tie 'prefer today' rule (gs_budget.go:185-189) already establishes the right intuition — extend it to near-ties, not just exact ones.
notes: From fantasy-baseball-strategist review, 2026-07-10 (finding #2). CLAUDE.md's GS-budget section has been corrected in this same session to describe the actual value-ranked gate (it previously described a since-superseded proportional-split formula).
close_reason: Fixed: estimated future GS demand now weighted by fractional share (not ceil-rounded to a full-value placeholder), and a 10% certainty margin extends the exact-tie today-preference rule to near-ties. Confirmed future starts unaffected. 3 new regression tests.

comments.author: Jon Nixon
comments.text:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** bug
**Summary:** The weekly game-start budget gate treats an uncertain, fractional future pitching-start estimate as a full-certainty competing claim on the remaining budget, which can cause it to suppress a today's confirmed start in favor of a future start that may never happen.

**Current behavior:**
When the gate needs to decide which scheduled starts to allow within a limited remaining weekly budget, it ranks all known and estimated starts by value and keeps the top N. For future days where the actual starters aren't announced yet, the code estimates a fractional expected number of starts (e.g., 0.2) based on roster composition, but then rounds that fractional estimate UP to a whole number of full-value placeholder entries before ranking. A day with only a 20% chance of producing a start is therefore treated as a certain, full-value start competing equally against today's already-confirmed starts.

**Desired behavior:**
An uncertain future start's claim on the budget should scale with its actual probability/fractional weight rather than being rounded up to a full competing entry. Today's confirmed, certain starts should have some degree of preference over equally-valued-but-uncertain future estimates, not just in exact ties.

**Key interfaces:**
- The budget-gate's future-demand forecasting, which already distinguishes "confirmed starters" from "estimated/fractional starters" per future day.
- The ranking step that decides which starts survive the budget cut.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] A future day's fractional/estimated start demand contributes to the ranking proportionally to its fractional value, not as one or more full-value placeholder entries.
- [ ] When a today start and a future estimated start are close in value (not just exactly equal), the gate prefers keeping today's start.
- [ ] Existing behavior for fully-confirmed starts (today and future) is unchanged.
- [ ] Existing test coverage for this gate is extended to cover a fractional-estimate scenario that would have previously over-suppressed a today start.

**Out of scope:**
- Changing how the roster-wide mean placeholder value itself is computed, or how confirmed future starters are weighted — only the fractional-vs-rounded-up treatment and near-tie preference are in scope.

title: Non-starter SP discount (0.10x) likely too generous for future-date/--matchup planning
description: internal/optimizer/pitcher_lineup.go:67 values a non-probable SP whose team plays at 10% of a full start. A projected-20-pt starter then shows as 2.0 pts, which can exceed a real RP's full projection and slot the SP (who most likely won't actually pitch that day) over an RP who will. Impact is muted for the daily production run (real probables are usually known, so real starters get full value), but it distorts --matchup/future-date planning where probables aren't yet announced. This is a repeat finding — flagged in a March 2026 audit and still unresolved.
acceptance_criteria: Lower the non-starter discount from 0.10x to something in the 0.03-0.05x range, or make it a function of how many days out the projection is (probables are announced ~1-5 days ahead for most teams; days further out should discount harder). Verify against backtest --matchup replays that the change doesn't regress lineups where the discount currently happens to work out.
notes: From fantasy-baseball-strategist review, 2026-07-10 (finding #3), confirmed unchanged since a March 2026 audit.
close_reason: Lowered non-starter SP discount from 0.10x to 0.05x (top of suggested 3-5% range), extracted as exported optimizer.NonStarterSPDiscount so backtest.go's mirrored discount can't drift independently (was a duplicated magic number). Regression test proves a 20pt/G non-starting SP no longer outranks a modest 1.1pt/G real RP. Note: did not build a full old-vs-new forward-planning replay harness (would require point-in-time projection snapshots this repo doesn't capture for what-if comparisons) - verified via unit regression test + full suite, not a live historical backtest replay.

comments.author: Jon Nixon
comments.text:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** The point-value discount applied to a pitcher who isn't a confirmed starter (but whose team is playing) is likely too generous when used for forward-looking/multi-day planning, letting a probably-not-pitching starter outrank a reliever who will definitely play.

**Current behavior:**
A non-confirmed starting pitcher is valued at a fixed 10% of their full-start projection. For same-day decisions this rarely matters because actual starters are usually confirmed by the time the lineup runs. For multi-day-ahead planning, where confirmed starters aren't yet known for later days, this 10% figure can put a high-projection starter's discounted value above a lower-projection but definitely-playing reliever's full value, resulting in a lineup slot given to a pitcher unlikely to actually play.

**Desired behavior:**
The discount for an unconfirmed starter should be low enough that it doesn't generally outrank a real, confirmed relief option, while still being nonzero so a genuinely likely starter isn't penalized to uselessness.

**Key interfaces:**
- The single constant/multiplier applied to a non-confirmed starter's projected value.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] The discount multiplier is reduced from its current value into a smaller range (informally, somewhere around 3-5% rather than 10% is a reasonable starting point, but pick a specific value and justify it).
- [ ] Replaying recent multi-day/matchup-period planning scenarios through the existing backtest tooling shows no regression in realized lineup points versus the current behavior.
- [ ] Same-day behavior (where starters are already confirmed) is unaffected, since confirmed starters don't go through this discount path at all.

**Out of scope:**
- Making the discount vary by how many days out the projection is (a fixed constant is fine for this pass) — that's a reasonable future refinement but a separate, larger change.

title: ATC RoS FanGraphs projections (atc-ros) return zero rows for both bat+pit — confirmed upstream 500, but pipeline has no visibility into it
description:
internal/projections.LoadPitcherProjections("atc-ros",...) and LoadBattingProjections("atc-ros",...) both return NoData=true / Len()=0 (confirmed live via a direct uncached package call, 2026-07-10). This is NOT a rosterbot bug: I loaded https://www.fangraphs.com/projections?type=ratc&stats=pit... and stats=bat... in a real browser and FanGraphs' OWN frontend makes the identical request (GET /api/projections?type=ratc&stats=bat|pit...) and gets a 500 from their own server for both. Our fgProjectionType mapping ('ratc') is correct; FanGraphs' backend is currently broken for the ATC RoS type (both roles), for reasons outside our control. steamer-ros, depthcharts-ros, and thebatx-ros all load fine (verified same test: Len 4667-5854 bat / 723-5854 pit range).

The real, fixable gap: the shadow/grade/report pipeline (internal/report's rankSystems, cmd/shadow.go's per-system capture) has no visibility into a system coming back with LoadResult.NoData=true. LoadPitcherProjections/LoadBattingProjections silently return an empty stub 'so the optimizer can still run' (correct for the live optimize path, which only ever uses one configured system) but shadow explicitly loops all 4 shadowSystems including atc-ros with no fallback (LoadPitcherProjections only chains RoS->preseason->CSV for a BASE system name; an explicit '-ros' input skips straight to a single API attempt then CSV). Per the model-auditor's independent M2 finding: report.rankSystems has no common-support/minimum-N guard, so a system silently grading projected=0 for every player can be misread as 'ATC is inaccurate' rather than 'ATC had no data that day' — actively misleading on the projection-site dashboard, and this has likely been happening silently every day since the outage started (Shadow's ECS task exits 0 regardless, since NoData isn't an error).
acceptance_criteria: 1) Re-check the FanGraphs endpoint periodically — this piece may self-resolve. 2) Add NoData visibility: either skip writing a shadow snapshot for a system+role that came back NoData (rather than writing a zero/empty one), or tag SnapshotPlayer with a HasProjection flag so report.rankSystems can exclude no-data systems from ranking instead of silently grading them as projected=0. 3) Surface a warning (log line or Pushover) when shadow's capture hits NoData for any of the 4 systems, so a future upstream outage on any system doesn't go unnoticed.
notes: Overlaps with fantasy-baseball-model-auditor's M2 finding (system-comparison ranking conflates coverage with accuracy) from the 2026-07-10 review — this issue supplies the confirmed concrete instance (atc-ros, both roles, upstream 500) plus root cause.

comments.author: Jon Nixon
comments.text:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** bug
**Summary:** The multi-system projection capture/comparison pipeline has no visibility when one of its FanGraphs projection systems returns no data, so a system-level outage silently produces misleading zero-value projections instead of a visible gap.

**Current behavior:**
When a projection system's API fetch returns no usable rows for a role (hitters or pitchers) — e.g. an upstream outage on the provider's side — the loader returns an empty-but-valid result rather than an error, by design (so the live optimizer, which only ever loads one configured system, degrades gracefully rather than crashing). The multi-system capture/comparison pipeline reuses this same loader for every system it captures, with no additional handling: it has no way to distinguish "this system genuinely has zero projected points for this player" (a real per-player fact) from "this system returned no data at all today" (a data-availability fact about the system). Downstream, whatever computes per-system accuracy rankings has no minimum-coverage guard, so a system with zero real data for a period can still be graded and ranked as if every player's true projection were zero.

**Desired behavior:**
A system-level "no data available" condition (as opposed to a legitimate per-player zero projection) should be distinguishable end to end: from the initial fetch, through whatever gets persisted for later grading, through the accuracy-ranking computation. A system that had no data for a given day/role should not be graded/ranked on that day/role at all — it should be excluded from that comparison rather than silently scored as if it projected zero points for everyone. Ranking logic that picks a "best" system for a given window/role should only consider systems that actually had data for a reasonable portion of that window, and should make it visible (in whatever the human-facing report/dashboard is) when a system is missing data rather than just omitting it silently.

**Key interfaces:**
- Whatever loader function fetches a named projection system's data returns a result that already distinguishes "empty because no data available" from "empty because this player has no projection" (an availability signal exists at the loader level today — the gap is that nothing downstream reads or propagates it).
- Whatever per-player record gets persisted for later grading (the projection "snapshot") should carry enough information to tell, in hindsight, whether the source system had real data for that player on that day, vs. a zero-filled placeholder.
- Whatever computes per-system accuracy/ranking across systems should skip a system for a given window/role when its data coverage for that window/role is effectively empty, rather than including it with a misleadingly perfect or terrible score.
- Whatever writes the daily multi-system capture should emit a visible warning (log line at minimum) when a system comes back with no data for a role, so a provider-side outage doesn't go unnoticed for an extended period.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] A day/role where a captured system had no real projection data is excluded from that system's accuracy ranking for that window, not graded as zero-point projections.
- [ ] The capture step logs (or otherwise surfaces) a clear warning when a system returns no data for a role, distinct from normal operation.
- [ ] Systems with sufficient real data are unaffected — no change to their grading/ranking behavior.
- [ ] Historical snapshots written before this change (which don't carry the new availability signal) are handled gracefully — treat them as "assume had data" so existing historical rankings don't silently change/disappear.

**Out of scope:**
- Fixing the actual upstream outage (external, not ours to fix) — confirmed via the provider's own site returning a server error for the same request, so this is transient/external.
- Adding retry/backoff logic for the upstream fetch itself.
- Changing which projection system the live (non-comparison) optimizer path uses — that path already only loads one system and isn't affected by this gap.

title: Harden optimize.go's period-number resolution to match gscheck's derivation
description:
Final whole-branch review of rosterbot-r10 (live Fantrax GS limits) found that cmd/optimize.go resolves the scoring-period number via GetMatchupWeekNumberForDate (a positional count over matchupWeekRanges' same-opponent date groupings), while internal/gscheck.RunGSCheck uses period.Number (parsed directly from Fantrax's own 'Scoring Period N' caption text). These are structurally independent derivations with no code-level guarantee of agreement -- matchupWeekRanges groups purely by consecutive same-opponent entries with no gap check, so a genuine back-to-back rematch against the same opponent would desync the position count from the real ScoringPeriod number, silently (GetGSLimits would still return valid data for the wrong period, no error/warning).

Verified directly against the live 2026 league (all 23 scoring periods this season, via GetScoringPeriodsAndTeams + GetMatchupWeekNumberForDate cross-check): zero mismatches, including period 16 (the All-Star break this whole effort was built for). So this does NOT manifest as a live bug this season -- filing as a hardening fast-follow, not a regression.

Suggested fix: source the period number in cmd/optimize.go's GS-budget block the same structurally-guaranteed way gscheck.go does -- via GetAllMatchupEntries()'s ScoringPeriod field (or an equivalent lookup keyed off today's date) instead of GetMatchupWeekNumberForDate's positional count. Before landing, verify MatchupEntry.ScoringPeriod is uniform across all entries within a single merged week (e.g. period 16's Jul13-26 span) -- not yet confirmed.
acceptance_criteria: Source the period number in cmd/optimize.go's GS-budget block the same structurally-guaranteed way internal/gscheck.RunGSCheck does -- via GetAllMatchupEntries()'s ScoringPeriod field (or an equivalent lookup keyed off today's date) instead of GetMatchupWeekNumberForDate's positional count. Before landing, verify MatchupEntry.ScoringPeriod is uniform across all entries within a single merged week (e.g. period 16's Jul13-26 span) -- not yet confirmed. Superseded in practice if rosterbot-z3b's Period Resolver lands first: consuming that resolver from cmd/optimize.go satisfies this issue directly, since both call sites would then share one derivation.
notes:
Resolved by earlier work, confirmed during rosterbot-z3b's code review (2026-07-12): the GS-budget block in cmd/optimize.go already sources its period number from ft.GetScoringPeriodsAndTeams() + fantrax.FindCurrentPeriod() -- the same structurally-guaranteed mechanism gscheck.go uses -- predating rosterbot-z3b's own changes. GetMatchupWeekNumberForDate/matchupWeekRanges (the positional-count derivation this issue described) is confirmed used only by internal/recap for an unrelated "matchup week number" label, never for period resolution, anywhere in the live codebase. The specific desync scenario this issue was filed for is provably impossible today.

Note: this did NOT happen via the literal mechanism this issue's acceptance criteria anticipated (consuming rosterbot-z3b's new fantrax.ResolvePeriod from the GS-budget block) -- z3b's reviewer judged that routing the already-correct, safety-critical GS-budget gate through a function with weaker fallback tiers would be a behavior change, not a genuine dedup, so it was deliberately left as-is. Closing on the substance (the described bug scenario is moot) rather than the anticipated mechanism.
close_reason: GS-budget block already sources period via the authoritative periods-list lookup (predates rosterbot-z3b); GetMatchupWeekNumberForDate confirmed unused for period resolution anywhere live. See notes.

title: Extract lineup-run orchestration out of cmd/optimize.go into a testable internal package
description: cmd/optimize.go is 1483 lines mixing CLI flag wiring with core orchestration: period resolution (resolveDatePeriod), the GS-budget gate, the projection pipeline, snapshot writing, and print/apply. cmd/shadow.go needs to redirect runOptimizes snapshot output to a per-system path; the only seam available is captureSystemRoot, a package-level mutable var declared in optimize.go and set/cleared from shadow.go. Two adapters genuinely exist (normal optimize run, shadow multi-system capture run) so the seam is real, but the mechanism is shared mutable state across files in the same package rather than an explicit parameter. Symptom of the logic never having left cmd/: cmd/optimize_period_test.go and cmd/optimize_snapshot_test.go test cmd-package internals directly, which is not where internal/ package tests normally live in this codebase. Architecture review (improve-codebase-architecture, 2026-07-12) flagged this as a shallow cmd-layer god-file plus a hacky global-var seam.
design: Move the run loop, resolveDatePeriod, and snapshot-writing logic into a new internal package (e.g. internal/lineuprun) behind a Run(Options) entry point, where Options carries an explicit SnapshotRoot field (replacing captureSystemRoot). cmd/optimize.go and cmd/shadow.go both shrink to: parse flags, build Options, call Run. The package-level captureSystemRoot var is deleted.
acceptance_criteria: internal/lineuprun (or equivalent) owns the orchestration with its own tests. captureSystemRoot global var is gone; SnapshotRoot is an explicit Options field set by both cmd/optimize.go and cmd/shadow.go. cmd/optimize_period_test.go and cmd/optimize_snapshot_test.go move to the new package (or are superseded by equivalent tests there). go test ./... passes. make run-all smoke test passes unchanged -- this is the hourly production entrypoint, so behavior must be identical, not just refactored.
notes: Filed from improve-codebase-architecture review, 2026-07-12. Larger blast radius than the period-resolver and blend-core issues since it touches the production hourly optimize entrypoint -- sequence after those land.

title: Extract shared blend-core from hitter/pitcher recency blending
description: internal/projections/blended.go (BlendedSource.GetPtsPerGame) and internal/projections/pitcher_blended.go (PitcherBlendedSource.GetPitcherPtsPerGame) independently implement the same shape: normalize name to player ID, look up recent Fantrax stats, branch on no-base-projection (raw recent value, no shrinkage), else blend base and recent with a dynamic weight derived from games played. rosterbot-4h7 already documents the same bug (unshrunk raw small-sample YTD trusted at 100 percent weight on the no-projection path) present identically in both files, because there is no shared module -- the logic was copied, not reused. Architecture review (improve-codebase-architecture, 2026-07-12) flagged this as duplicated complexity: deleting either file would force the other to reproduce the same lookup/fallback/shrink shape, which is the deletion test for real duplication.
design: Extract a shared blend-core function parameterized by a stabilization constant, a base floor, and a recent-lookup callback (or equivalent shared internal helper in internal/projections). BlendedSource and PitcherBlendedSource become thin adapters supplying their role-specific constants (hitterStabilizationPA/hitterBaseFloor vs spStabilizationGP/rpStabilizationGP/pitcherBaseFloor) and call the shared core instead of re-implementing the branch structure.
acceptance_criteria: One shared blend-core implementation exists; BlendedSource.GetPtsPerGame and PitcherBlendedSource.GetPitcherPtsPerGame both delegate to it. The no-projection-fallback shrinkage fix from rosterbot-4h7 is implemented once in the shared core and applies to both hitters and pitchers. Existing hitter and pitcher blending tests pass unchanged (or are updated to exercise the shared core directly).
notes: Filed from improve-codebase-architecture review, 2026-07-12. Do this extraction as the fix vehicle for rosterbot-4h7 rather than patching blended.go and pitcher_blended.go identically.

title: Manual-trigger API (POST /v1/jobs/{name}) has no in-flight check, can race scheduled EventBridge runs
description: POST /v1/jobs/{name} (infra/infra.go, ecs:RunTask launch handler ~line 218-234) launches a task on demand with no check for an already-running instance of the same job. Mostly benign since cache writes are last-write-wins, EXCEPT the claims cursor (internal/claims/run.go, persisted at .waivers/last-claims.json via CLAIMS_CURSOR_PATH) -- an overlapping manual + scheduled run there can both read the same cursor before either writes, producing duplicate Pushover notifications for the same claim events. Cosmetic (no data loss, no incorrect ledger state), but a real latent race. Per the Database Optimizer subagent's 2026-07-03 AWS storage audit (finding #3 of 3).
acceptance_criteria: Before ecs:RunTask in the manual-trigger handler, check GET /v1/runs (or the equivalent run-ledger query) for an in-flight run of the same job name and reject/no-op the manual trigger (with a clear API response) if one is already running. Verify by manually triggering the claims job while a scheduled run is in flight and confirming only one Pushover notification fires.
close_reason: Added an in-flight check to POST /v1/jobs/{name} (internal/lineupapi/handler.go handleJob + jobs.go inFlightRun): before launching, scans recent runs for a RUNNING entry whose command's first token matches the requested job name, and rejects with 409 if found. Matches on base job name (ignoring flags) per the acceptance criteria's 'same job name' wording, since the race (e.g. claims cursor) isn't specific to one param combination. Bounded by a 2h staleness window since entrypoint.sh only flips RUNNING->SUCCESS/FAILED after the command exits -- a hard crash mid-run would otherwise leave a ledger entry RUNNING forever and permanently block future manual triggers for that job, which would have been a worse regression than the race it fixes. Fails open (nil RunStore or a List error skips the check) since this is a race-reduction guard, not a strict lock, and local  runs without Runs configured at all. 8 new tests covering the pure inFlightRun logic (name matching, staleness, fail-open paths) plus full handler-level 409/202 wiring.

title: Add S3 lifecycle rule to expire noncurrent cache-object versions in StateBucket
description: StateBucket (infra/infra.go:45-48) has S3 versioning enabled with no lifecycle rule expiring noncurrent versions. Every overwrite of a cache/ key (the FileCache Store's S3 adapter, see cache-store-seam) leaves the prior version live forever, so storage cost for the cache/ prefix grows unbounded relative to actual live cache size. Already flagged as a gap in docs/aws-deployment.md but never shipped, per the Database Optimizer subagent's 2026-07-03 AWS storage audit (finding #2 of 3).
acceptance_criteria: Add a LifecycleRule (CDK, infra/infra.go) scoped to the cache/ prefix that expires noncurrent versions after 7-14 days. Leave analysis/, backtest/, runs/ (and runledger/), lineup/, session/, and claims/ untouched -- those prefixes are intentionally durable/bounded (append-only archives or small ledgers), unlike cache/ which is TTL-driven and safe to trim. Verify via cdk diff that only the new lifecycle rule changes, and confirm post-deploy (AWS console or aws s3api get-bucket-lifecycle-configuration).
close_reason: Added a LifecycleRule to StateBucket (infra/infra.go) scoped to the cache/ prefix, expiring noncurrent versions after 14 days (top of the suggested 7-14d range). Verified via cdk diff -c enableBuild=true against live AWS: the only resource change is the new LifecycleConfiguration on StateBucket (plus an expected Lambda code-hash bump from the unrelated rosterbot-744 fix already on this branch) -- no other resources touched, confirming analysis/, backtest/, runs/, lineup/, session/, and claims/ prefixes are unaffected. NOT deployed -- cdk deploy is a real infra change and needs explicit go-ahead. Incidentally found and fixed pre-existing dependency drift in lambda/go.mod (root module had a dependabot bump that was never re-tidied into the lambda submodule) which was blocking cdk diff's asset bundling; unrelated to this issue but needed to unblock verification.

title: Legacy Analysis Store partitions (pre-system dimension) invisible to Athena ad-hoc queries
description:
internal/analysis/reader.go's SystemFromKey/LegacySystem handles pre-migration dt=X/grades.ndjson keys (no system= segment) so the HTML projection dashboard reads them correctly via the Go reader. But the Athena partition-projection template in infra/infra.go (storage.location.template, ~line 318) only matches dt=${dt}/system=${system}/, so ad-hoc SQL against early dates (before the system dimension was added) undercounts vs. what the dashboard shows.

From the Database Optimizer subagent's 2026-07-03 AWS storage audit (finding #1 of 3 -- the other two are filed as separate bd issues).

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Identify a dt= date range that predates the system= partition dimension being added to analysis/grades/ (pre-migration writes at analysis/grades/dt=X/grades.ndjson, no system= segment).
2. Run an Athena SELECT COUNT(*) FROM rosterbot_analysis.grades WHERE dt BETWEEN <pre-migration-range> against the live table.
3. Compare against the row count the HTML projection dashboard shows for the same date range (it reads via internal/analysis.Reader, which does handle the legacy no-system= keys through SystemFromKey/LegacySystem).
4. Observe Athena undercounts relative to the dashboard, because the partition-projection template only matches dt=${dt}/system=${system}/ and never registers the legacy dt=${dt}/ (no system=) partitions.
acceptance_criteria: Decide and implement one of: (a) backfill the legacy dt=X/grades.ndjson objects into system=depthcharts-ros/ partitions (the system the bot actually ran pre-migration, i.e. analysis.LegacySystem) so Athena and the dashboard agree, or (b) explicitly document the gap (e.g. in docs/aws-deployment.md or an Athena table comment) so nobody is surprised by an Athena/dashboard mismatch on early dates. No automated Athena consumers exist today (interactive queries only), so this is a data-hygiene/documentation call, not an active production bug.

title: No-projection blend fallback trusts raw 2-GP YTD at 100% weight
description:
internal/projections/blended.go:59-65 and pitcher_blended.go:66-72: when a player has no FanGraphs projection but has recent Fantrax stats, the blend returns recent.FPtsPerGame UNSHRUNK at 100% weight, gated only by GamesPlayed >= minGP (BLEND_MIN_GP, default 2). The shrinkage formula (seasonWeight = approxPA/(approxPA+250)) only applies on the path where a base projection also exists. So a just-called-up player who homers twice in 2 games gets projected as a season-long standout; scoreRoster (internal/optimizer/lineup.go:265-268) feeds that straight into the optimizer and the snapshot. Cold 2-game players are correctly benched near-zero, but hot 2-game players can be started over proven options — asymmetric risk concentrated exactly in the fringe/call-up population that waiver and streaming decisions hinge on.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Pick a player with no FanGraphs depthcharts-ros projection (a recent call-up) who went 2-for-4 with 2 HR in their first 2 MLB games.
2. Run the optimizer for a date after those 2 games with --projections depthcharts-ros (or call BlendedSource.GetPtsPerGame / PitcherBlendedSource.GetPitcherPtsPerGame directly in a unit test).
3. Observe the returned FP/G equals the raw 2-game recent.FPtsPerGame at full weight, with no shrinkage toward a league/positional baseline.
4. Compare to a player with an established 90+ GP sample and no base projection: their recent.FPtsPerGame is also returned unshrunk today, but the fix should leave that case materially unaffected while pulling the 2-game case toward a baseline.
acceptance_criteria: Regress the standalone recent value toward a positional/league-average baseline using the same n/(n+k) shrinkage idea (prior = league-avg FP/G at that position) instead of trusting it at 100%, or raise the projection-less minGP threshold to ~10-15 GP before treating raw YTD as a full projection.

comments.author: Jon Nixon
comments.text:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** bug
**Summary:** When a player has no season-long base projection available (e.g. a recent call-up) but does have a couple of games of recorded performance, that raw small-sample performance is used as their full point projection at 100% confidence, with no regression toward a more reasonable baseline — creating small-sample mirages that can distort start/sit and waiver decisions specifically for the fringe/recently-added player population where this matters most.

**Current behavior:**
The blending logic has two paths: when a player has both a base projection and recent performance data, recent performance is blended in with a weight that grows gradually with sample size (more games played → more trust in recent performance, per an established shrinkage formula). But when a player has no base projection at all, the fallback path skips this shrinkage entirely and uses the raw recent-performance rate directly, gated only by a low minimum-games threshold. A player with an outlier 1-2 game stretch (e.g., two home runs in two games) gets projected as if that rate would continue, rather than being pulled toward a realistic baseline.

**Desired behavior:**
The no-base-projection fallback path should apply comparable regression/shrinkage logic to what the has-base-projection path already does, so a tiny sample doesn't get trusted as a stable rate. A reasonable prior to regress toward is a league- or position-average rate, or simply the same shrinkage math promoted to a higher minimum sample size before trusting the raw rate at anything close to full weight.

**Key interfaces:**
- The no-base-projection fallback branch in both the hitter and pitcher blending logic (this pattern exists in both places and should be fixed consistently in both).

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] A player with only a handful of games and no base projection is no longer assigned their raw small-sample rate at full weight; their projection is pulled toward a reasonable baseline that loosens as their sample size grows.
- [ ] A player with a large, well-established recent sample (but still no base projection, e.g. a full season with no available upstream projection) is largely unaffected — the fix should target small samples specifically, not penalize established performers who happen to lack a projection.
- [ ] Existing test coverage for the blending logic is extended to cover a small-sample no-base-projection scenario.

**Out of scope:**
- Changing the shrinkage formula used on the has-base-projection path (only the no-projection fallback path is in scope) — see the separate open item about whether that main blend formula double-counts season form, which is a distinct, larger question still being validated.

title: Repoint go-fantrax replace at pmurley/go-fantrax once GAMES_PER_POS PR merges
description: rosterbot-r10 temporarily points the go-fantrax replace directive at the nixon-commits/go-fantrax add-games-per-position-endpoint branch (see go.mod). Once https://github.com/pmurley/go-fantrax/pull/7 merges to pmurley/go-fantrax main, run: go mod edit -replace github.com/pmurley/go-fantrax=github.com/nixon-commits/go-fantrax@main (or drop the replace and bump the require version if depending on pmurley/go-fantrax directly is preferred), then go mod tidy, go build ./..., go test ./..., commit.
acceptance_criteria: Once https://github.com/pmurley/go-fantrax/pull/7 merges to pmurley/go-fantrax main: run 'go mod edit -replace github.com/pmurley/go-fantrax=github.com/nixon-commits/go-fantrax@main' (or drop the replace and bump the require version directly if depending on pmurley/go-fantrax main is preferred), then 'go mod tidy', 'go build ./...', 'go test ./...', and commit. go.mod no longer points at the temporary nixon-commits fork branch; all tests and the build pass unchanged.

title: Move run ledger to its own S3 prefix (runledger/) instead of sharing runs/
description:
Follow-up to the /v1/runs pagination fix (PR #27, internal/lineupapi/s3lineup/runs.go). Root cause of that bug: the run ledger (runs/<invts>-<id>.json, listed by RunsStore.recent()) shares the runs/ S3 prefix with per-run output blobs (runs/<hex-id>/output.json, written by OutputStore). Lexicographic key ordering interleaves the two, so listing the ledger requires paginating past an ever-growing pool of unrelated output keys.

PR #27 fixed this on the reader side (recent() now follows NextContinuationToken instead of assuming a single page). That's correct and already deployed, but every /v1/runs and /v1/runs/{id} call still pages through however many output sub-keys happen to sort before the ledger records it needs - cost grows with total run count, not with `limit`.

This ticket is to actually separate the two into distinct prefixes so ledger listing goes back to being a cheap, bounded operation with no dependency on how many output blobs exist.
design:
- Add a new prefix (e.g. runledger/) for ledger records only; leave OutputStore on runs/<id>/output.json (output blobs are looked up by exact key via Get(), never listed, so they don't need to move).
- Update both call sites that construct RunsStore with s3lineup.NewRuns(ctx, bucket, "runs/"):
  - cmd/ledger.go:79 (the run-ledger CLI writer, PutRun)
  - lambda/main.go:42 (the read path, List/Get)
  Change both to "runledger/".
- One-time migration: copy existing runs/<invts>-<id>.json objects (NOT the runs/<hex-id>/output.json sub-objects) to runledger/<invts>-<id>.json in STATE_BUCKET. Can be a small one-off Go script or `aws s3api list-objects-v2` + filtered `aws s3 cp` loop (must exclude keys containing "/" after the runs/ prefix, matching the existing flat-key filter logic in RunsStore.recent()).
- After migration is verified (both old runs/*.json ledger keys and new runledger/*.json exist and /v1/runs still reports correctly against the new prefix), old runs/*.json ledger records can be left in place (harmless, no longer listed) or cleaned up later - not urgent since they're small flat JSON objects.
- Once runledger/ is confirmed to contain only flat ledger keys, recent() no longer strictly needs the "/" sub-key filter or even the pagination loop for correctness (only for scaling past 1000 total ledger records) - but there's no harm in leaving both, they're now just defensive rather than load-bearing.
- Verify old and new list against each other during cutover (e.g. run both prefixes' List side by side for a day) before removing the "runs/" writer path.
acceptance_criteria:
- Ledger writer (cmd/ledger.go) and reader (lambda/main.go) both point at runledger/ prefix
- Existing ledger records migrated from runs/*.json to runledger/*.json with no data loss (spot-check record count and a handful of IDs match)
- GET /v1/runs and /v1/runs/{id} verified live against the new prefix, newest-first, matching pre-migration output
- go test ./... passes with updated s3lineup tests reflecting the new prefix
close_reason: Shipped in #28 (merged aec08ed). Writer (cmd/ledger.go) and reader (lambda/main.go) both on runledger/; infra IAM updated; 409 ledger records migrated from runs/ to runledger/ (verified: independent count match 409=409, byte-identical spot check); GET /v1/runs and /v1/runs/{id} verified live post-deploy, newest-first. Follow-up rosterbot-q3b filed for optional cleanup of the now-orphaned old runs/*.json ledger keys.

title: Share HKB player-enrichment lookup between claims and transactions
description: internal/claims/hkb.go (lookupHKB, building a SidePlayer) and internal/transactions/transactions.go (the TradePlayer builder) independently map an hkb.Player record onto a near-identical enriched struct -- Rank, Value, Trend30D/ValueChange30D, Level, Prospect, IsPitcher, ERA/WHIP or OPS depending on pitcher-vs-hitter -- both keyed by playername.Normalize(name) lookup into a map built from internal/hkb data. transactions.go even has a normalizeName wrapper that just calls playername.Normalize directly. Architecture review (improve-codebase-architecture, 2026-07-12) flagged this as real duplication by the deletion test: deleting either lookup function would force the other package to reproduce the same field-by-field mapping.
design: Move the enrichment mapping into internal/hkb (already the leaf package that owns HKB data) as a shared function, e.g. hkb.Enrich(name, position string, lookup map[string]hkb.Player) returning a shared enriched-player value. internal/claims and internal/transactions both call it instead of maintaining their own copy; their SidePlayer/TradePlayer types either become thin aliases of the shared shape or embed it.
acceptance_criteria: One enrichment function lives in internal/hkb. internal/claims/hkb.go and internal/transactions/transactions.go both call it; the duplicated field-mapping logic is deleted from at least one of the two packages. Existing claims and transactions tests pass unchanged.
notes: Filed from improve-codebase-architecture review, 2026-07-12. Lowest priority of the four candidates from that review -- small, mechanical, low risk.

title: CYC/NH/PG bonus stats unhandled in MLB game-log backfill scorer
description: CYC (cycle) is a declared league scoring category but is absent from ApplyHitter's statMap (internal/scoring/hitter.go:36-42); NH (no-hitter) and PG (perfect game, listed at 25/75 pts in strategist's review) are similarly absent from ApplyPitcher. Correctly omitted from projections (unprojectable event-level bonuses), and self-cancels in grading since both projected and actual sides omit them equally. The only real gap is internal/fantrax/mlb_backfill.go's single-game log scorer (used by recap/backtest for a waiver pickup's first-day FPts), which COULD detect these from one game's box line (cycle = >=1 each of 1B/2B/3B/HR in a game; no-hitter/perfect game from H/BB/HBP/ER over 9 IP) but currently doesn't, so a backfilled day containing one of these events under-counts FPts by the bonus amount.
acceptance_criteria: Add cycle/no-hitter/perfect-game detection to the mlb_backfill.go single-game scorer so historical grading is exact. Confirm the league's actual Fantrax CYC/NH/PG weights first (bd notes both agents flagged this independently but neither confirmed the live weight value).
notes: From both fantasy-baseball-model-auditor (L1) and fantasy-baseball-strategist (finding #5) reviews, 2026-07-10. Very low real-world frequency (~2-3 cycles/year leaguewide); lowest priority of this review's findings.

comments.author: Jon Nixon
comments.text:
> *This was generated by AI during triage.*

## Agent Brief

**Category:** enhancement
**Summary:** Rare in-game bonus events (a batter hitting for the cycle, a pitcher throwing a no-hitter or perfect game) aren't detected when reconstructing a player's historical single-day fantasy point total from raw box-score-style game logs, undercounting that day's points by the bonus amount whenever one of these rare events occurs.

**Current behavior:**
The league's scoring rules include bonus point values for these three rare single-game achievements. The regular projection and live-scoring paths correctly don't attempt to project them (they're unprojectable rare events). But the historical single-game reconstruction path — used to backfill a day's fantasy points from raw stats when needed (e.g. for a player's first day after a waiver pickup) — also doesn't check for them, even though it has access to the full single-game stat line needed to detect all three.

**Desired behavior:**
The historical single-game reconstruction path should detect all three events from the game's stat line and add the corresponding bonus points, so historical point totals are exactly correct even on the rare day one of these occurs.

**Key interfaces:**
- The single-game stat-line-to-points scorer used by the historical backfill path.
- Cycle detection needs at least one single, double, triple, and home run in the same game.
- No-hitter/perfect-game detection needs innings-pitched, hits-allowed, walks-allowed, and hit-by-pitch-allowed (perfect game additionally requires zero baserunners by any means) over a minimum of 9 innings.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] Before implementing, confirm the league's actual configured point values for these three categories (they may already be zero, in which case this is a no-op and the issue should just be closed with that finding).
- [ ] If non-zero, a synthetic game line representing each of the three events is correctly detected and scores the bonus.
- [ ] A normal game log with none of these events is unaffected.

**Out of scope:**
- Adding these to the season-long or per-game projection paths (they remain intentionally unprojectable) — only the historical single-game backfill/reconstruction path is in scope.

title: Delete stale runs/*.json ledger records after runledger/ migration
description: rosterbot-432 migrated 409 run-ledger records from runs/*.json to runledger/*.json (both writer and reader now point at runledger/, verified live). The old runs/*.json ledger objects were deliberately left in place per the migration design (harmless, no longer listed by RunsStore -- only runs/<id>/output.json sub-keys are still read there). This is optional cleanup to reclaim the ~409 stale flat objects once nobody needs to cross-reference the old location; not urgent.
acceptance_criteria: Old flat runs/<invts>-<id>.json ledger keys (as opposed to runs/<id>/output.json sub-keys, which must be preserved) are deleted from the live STATE_BUCKET; runs/<id>/output.json objects and GET /v1/runs are unaffected.
